clos.text: Define the five essential components of an agent (LLM, tools, memory, loop, constitutional prompt) and explain the mechanical role each plays in decision-making and execution.
Distinguish between predetermined and model-driven control flow, and use this distinction to classify a system as a workflow or agent.
Apply the escalation principle to justify whether a given problem requires an agent or can be solved by a simpler point on the spectrum (single call, tool-augmented LLM, or workflow).
Map any agentic system to its Layer 1 patterns (reasoning, tool use, memory, control), Layer 2 composition (single agent, multi-agent), and characteristic Layer 3 failure modes.
Quantify the three costs of adding agency—latency, tokens, and failure modes—for a given architectural choice.
Implement a working agent with a constitutional prompt, think-act-observe loop, and at least one MCP tool, demonstrating model-driven control flow.
Interpret agent run metrics (iterations, tool calls, termination status) to assess whether a workflow or autonomous design is more suitable for a problem.
Evaluate how a constitutional prompt shapes agent behavior and justify design choices (e.g., forbidding stubs or confabulated APIs) in terms of reliability and scientific validity.

topics.name: Agent definition and anatomy: LLM, tools, memory, loop, constitutional prompt
The spectrum of agency: single call, tool-augmented LLM, workflow, single agent, multi-agent
Workflow vs. agent: predetermined vs. model-driven control flow
The escalation principle: simplest viable architecture first, earned complexity
Cost-of-every-pattern lens: latency, tokens, failure modes
Three-layer course model: Layer 1 patterns, Layer 2 composition, Layer 3 anti-patterns
Six-step procedure for analyzing any agentic system
Agent loop implementation: think-act-observe, MCP tools, termination logic
Constitutional prompts: behavioral specs, standards enforcement, persona design
Workflow vs. autonomous design tradeoffs: control, predictability, cost, debuggability

guiding_principles: Emphasize rigorous engineering discipline over hype. Complexity must be earned through demonstrated failure of simpler designs. When teaching agent patterns, present them as cost–benefit tradeoffs: quantify latency, token overhead, and failure modes explicitly. Favor analysis over memorization—students should practice the six-step system-reading procedure on real examples and make architectural choices defensible by escalation-principle reasoning, not by what looks impressive in a slide deck.
